caveats: a: Four-digit rubber-product employment is not retread-specific.
a: Safety-critical casing judgment may be assistable but not fully substitutable.
rho: NHTSA requirements show accountability but not implementation rates.
rho: Truck and passenger retreads face different process and regulatory conditions.
e: The NAICS definition does not distinguish captive from external operations.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed eligible count.
s5: The evidence is cross-industry intention rather than completed deal flow.
s5: Chain acquisitions and dealer transactions may not be coded as 326212.
q: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
q: Local small fleets likely offer greater retention than national fleets or dealer networks.
d5: VMT is not retread volume and light-duty vehicles use few retreads.
d5: EPA fuel-saving verification supports customer value, not total market demand.
o: The estimate is inferred from the physical process and accountability requirements.
o: New-tire substitution affects d5 more than operator requirement and is not double-counted here.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B service and product revenue per retread, casing, or tire-management program, often tied to fleet routes and replacement cycles, with pickup and delivery, casing acceptance, tread choice, warranty, and volume pricing.
lens.excluded_activities: New-tire manufacturing, tire repair or installation without retreading, retail-only dealers, captive fleet shops not serving external customers, casing brokers without processing, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market tire retreaders and rebuilders that repeatedly inspect customer or program casings, apply new tread, test and identify finished retreads, and supply external trucking fleets, bus operators, dealers, off-road users, and tire-management programs.

a.bridge: BLS occupation and wage data cover broader NAICS 326200 Rubber Product Manufacturing rather than retreading tasks. I map casing intake and imaging, inspection support, production scheduling, route and inventory planning, quality documentation, maintenance triage, sales, billing, and administration into exposed wages; larger new-tire plants in the proxy likely make it understate the service and logistics share but overstate scalable automation resources.
caveats: Four-digit rubber-product employment is not retread-specific.
Safety-critical casing judgment may be assistable but not fully substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and current-automation study across 12-15 independent retread plants, including casing rejection decisions, inspection time, buffing and building labor, QA, logistics, sales, billing, and fleet reporting.
a.rationale: Retreading is production-heavy, yet each casing requires intake, inspection, disposition, tracking, process records, and fleet coordination. AI-assisted imaging, anomaly detection, scheduling, route planning, documentation, and administrative workflow create a meaningful but bounded opportunity.

d5.bridge: FHWA forecasts total and light-duty vehicle miles, a tire-wear driver, not retread units; EPA verifies fuel-saving retread technologies but does not forecast adoption. I translate gradual travel growth plus fleet cost and sustainability incentives into modest retread demand growth, while new-tire pricing, casing quality, imports, fleet mix, and retread acceptance can push realized demand below the proxy.
caveats: VMT is not retread volume and light-duty vehicles use few retreads.
EPA fuel-saving verification supports customer value, not total market demand.
d5.change_evidence: US retread unit volumes by truck, bus, aviation, and off-road segment; fleet mileage and replacement forecasts; new-versus-retread price spreads; casing rejection rates; and procurement policies of large fleets.
d5.rationale: Ongoing truck and fleet mileage creates repeat tire wear, and retreads can lower lifecycle and fuel costs when verified products are used. Demand remains exposed to freight cycles, cheap new imports, casing availability and quality, single-use tire design, fleet safety policy, and tire-life improvement.

caveats: The NAICS definition does not distinguish captive from external operations.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed eligible count.
e.change_evidence: A plant-by-plant owner and customer map using Census, D&B, dealer networks, and industry directories, then verification of external revenue, program recurrence, captive status, EBITDA, concentration, equipment, compliance, and casing supply.
e.rationale: Retreading usually serves external fleets or dealer networks repeatedly and is often operated regionally, fitting the recurring outsourced lens better than new-tire manufacturing. Exclusions remain for captive shops, thin or declining volumes, unsafe or obsolete equipment, excessive concentration, environmental liabilities, weak casing supply, and firms outside normalized LMM economics.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326212 ($367,603,570) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326212 ($1,613,763,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $256,805 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326212); class <5: 77 firms, avg revenue/firm $439,468 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,382 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,664,594 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $243,863 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,294,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $482,644 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,404,143 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,084,707 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,026,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,054,919 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,794,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,097,419 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 91

caveats: The estimate is inferred from the physical process and accountability requirements.
New-tire substitution affects d5 more than operator requirement and is not double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Fleet insourcing and no-retread policy trends, autonomous inspection and production evidence, casingless or non-pneumatic tire adoption, and program renewal versus new-tire substitution data.
o.rationale: Customers still require an accountable operator to inspect each used casing, prepare it, apply and cure tread, test the finished tire, identify the retreader, manage warranty, and deliver it. Software can assist decisions and records but cannot provide the physical retread; fleet insourcing and substitution to new tires are the main displacement routes.

caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
Local small fleets likely offer greater retention than national fleets or dealer networks.
q.change_evidence: Fleet agreements, pricing and rebate schedules, renewal and churn histories, bid cadence, cost-per-mile guarantees, warranty credits, route economics, and margins before and after workflow changes.
q.rationale: Route density, casing history, turnaround, rejection discipline, warranty performance, and fleet reporting can make the service sticky and allow retention. Large fleets and dealer networks bid programs, benchmark cost per mile, and expect productivity sharing, so savings are partly competed away over renewals.

caveats: NHTSA requirements show accountability but not implementation rates.
Truck and passenger retreads face different process and regulatory conditions.
rho.change_evidence: Completed deployments of shearography or vision inspection, measured false-accept and false-reject rates, retread management-system adoption, integrator costs, paybacks, and post-implementation labor hours from independent plants.
rho.rationale: Scheduling, route, documentation, billing, and customer-reporting tools are deployable within five years. Inspection vision and process analytics require labeled defect data, equipment integration, validation, and human escalation; small plants face capex, workflow, training, and safety-accountability constraints.

s5.bridge: Gallup observes five-year sell or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners generally, not completed control transfers among eligible retreaders. I center near the survey's 22 percent employer intention, with a range reflecting family transfers, closures, dealer-chain consolidation, non-completion, and the possibility that mature regional owners are older than the general population.
caveats: The evidence is cross-industry intention rather than completed deal flow.
Chain acquisitions and dealer transactions may not be coded as 326212.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of retread transaction data from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, dealer and tire-industry advisers, matched to the eligible denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: Regional and family ownership makes succession plausible, and the activity can transfer with plant, accounts, casing relationships, routes, and workforce. Some intended exits will instead become closures, internal transitions, or tuck-ins without a standalone transferable platform.

principal_driver: Recurring fleet tire cycles and sticky local programs create repeat work with optimizable inspection, routing, and production support.
principal_weakness: Safety-critical casing judgment and physical processing limit substitution, while sophisticated fleets pressure pricing.
